sourcePolicy: Official GitHub repository, file, commit, issue, release, and API metadata only.

rationales.customerRelevance: The first-party runnable example is a real multi-agent financial research workflow with planner, parallel search, writer, specialist tools, verifier, and bounded revisions.
rationales.noSecretReproducibility: The official manager test subclasses the orchestration methods and exercises the manager deterministically without invoking a hosted model; the measured manager boundary therefore needs no paid API or secret.
rationales.measurableFailureSurface: The manager exposes automatable search-tool failure, parallel result aggregation, structured verifier output, retry exhaustion, and graceful-failure outcomes with explicit call/count oracles.
rationales.maintenanceAdoption: The repository is non-archived, had a maintainer commit one day before retrieval, published v0.13.5 three days before retrieval, and shows 3,429 stars and 864 forks in GitHub metadata.
rationales.licenseClarity: GitHub identifies the repository license as MIT and the root LICENSE file provides a direct attribution path.
rationales.overnightScopeability: The target is a two-file manager/test slice with an existing deterministic subclass seam and a focused package script, so setup and evidence remain bounded.

candidates.targetSlice: packages/agent local runtime and mock-backed journeys

rationales.customerRelevance: Eliza is a shipped local-first agent application with chat, memory, tools, browser/computer use, automations, and an explicit agent runtime.
rationales.noSecretReproducibility: The official repository documents local models and a mock cloud stack, and contains mock-backed agent journey tests, but meaningful app setup is a large Bun monorepo with heavyweight optional artifacts.
rationales.measurableFailureSurface: First-party tests and runtime primitives expose action routing, memory lifecycle, plugin failures, multi-turn state, and user-journey outcomes with observable local boundaries.
rationales.maintenanceAdoption: The repository is non-archived, had same-day maintainer activity at retrieval, and GitHub metadata shows 18,768 stars and 5,579 forks.
rationales.licenseClarity: GitHub and the repository README identify a root MIT license with a direct license file.
rationales.overnightScopeability: The active application and runtime span a very large Bun monorepo, native targets, plugins, and generated artifacts, making a defensible overnight slice costly.

rationales.customerRelevance: Agent TARS is a shipped CLI/Web UI agent application using multimodal model decisions, browser/computer tools, MCP, and an event stream.
rationales.noSecretReproducibility: The official core contains recorded request, response, tool-call, and event-stream snapshots, but normal agent execution requires a model provider and API key; only the snapshot boundary is secret-free.
rationales.measurableFailureSurface: Recorded event streams and tool-call snapshots expose multi-step sequencing, tool dispatch, malformed events, and terminal outcomes with automatable comparisons.
rationales.maintenanceAdoption: The repository is non-archived, had a maintainer commit within three weeks of retrieval, a release within the prior year, and strong GitHub star/fork signals.
rationales.licenseClarity: GitHub identifies Apache-2.0 and the root license provides clear reuse and attribution terms.
rationales.overnightScopeability: A snapshot-backed core slice is bounded, but the multimodal runtime, browser tooling, pnpm workspace, and recorded fixtures add setup and interpretation cost.

rationales.customerRelevance: Open SWE is a real asynchronous coding agent with tools, subagents, message injection, failure middleware, persistent sandboxes, and external invocation surfaces.
rationales.noSecretReproducibility: Individual middleware can be unit-isolated, but the documented core application relies on LangGraph services, cloud sandboxes, GitHub integration, and optional Slack or Linear credentials.
rationales.measurableFailureSurface: The official middleware layout exposes tool errors, queue consistency, model fallback, task retry, timeouts, state repair, and graceful notification behavior.
rationales.maintenanceAdoption: The repository is non-archived, had a maintainer commit three days before retrieval, and GitHub metadata shows 10,355 stars and 1,175 forks.
rationales.licenseClarity: The repository README and root license clearly identify MIT licensing and attribution.
rationales.overnightScopeability: A credible target slice crosses a large Python application, LangGraph middleware, sandbox providers, and external-service interfaces, so setup is not overnight-friendly.

rationales.customerRelevance: Sim is a shipped agent and workflow application with tool integrations, memory-like data surfaces, scheduled runs, logs, and an agent execution block.
rationales.noSecretReproducibility: Self-hosting is available and local model support is documented, but the application setup requires Docker, PostgreSQL with pgvector, generated secrets, and substantial services; chat also needs a hosted key.
rationales.measurableFailureSurface: The workflow engine offers automatable agent block routing, tool output, integration failure, scheduling, state, and terminal workflow result surfaces.
rationales.maintenanceAdoption: The repository is non-archived, had a maintainer release commit two days before retrieval, released v0.7.39, and has strong GitHub adoption signals.
rationales.licenseClarity: GitHub and the repository README identify Apache-2.0 with a root license file.
rationales.overnightScopeability: The relevant execution slice sits inside a large Bun monorepo with database, Docker, application, and integration setup, making a rigorous isolated teardown expensive.
